request.question: Which was founded earlier, The Week or New Scientist?

title: Massachusetts Audubon Society
Pirate Party (Italy)
Jordan River Foundation
Champ de Mars Racecourse
Canons Regular of the Holy Sepulchre
The Week
Nominative determinism
Gloucester Old Bank
New Scientist
1883 Philadelphia Quakers season
sentences: The Massachusetts Audubon Society, founded in 1896 by Harriet Hemenway and headquartered in Lincoln, Massachusetts, is a nonprofit organization dedicated to "protecting the nature of Massachusetts."
Mass Audubon is independent of the National Audubon Society, and in fact was founded earlier.
Mass Audubon protects 36,500 acres of land throughout Massachusetts, saving birds and other wildlife, and making nature accessible to all with its wildlife sanctuaries and 20 nature centers.
The Italian Pirate Party (Italian: "Partito Pirata Italiano" ) is a political party in Italy, founded on 16 September 2006, modelled on the Pirate Party of Sweden, founded earlier that year.
It supports reform of copyright and patent law, privacy and freedom of expression.

The "Jordan River Foundation" was founded earlier by Queen Nour Al Hussein, the wife of the late king of Jordan Hussein bin Talal, after the death of king Hussein, queen Nour left her position as the Chair person of "Jordan River Foundation" and the new queen Rania took over the position.
The Champ de Mars Racecourse is a thoroughbred horse race track in Port Louis, Mauritius.

The Racecourse was inaugurated on 25 June 1812, by The Mauritius Turf Club (MTC) which was founded earlier in the same year by Sir Robert Townsend Farquhar, who was the first British Governor of Mauritius.
The Mauritius Turf Club is the oldest horse-racing club in the Southern Hemisphere and the second oldest in the world.
The race track follows a very selective right hand oval path and is relatively small in size, with a circumference of 1,298 meters (4,258.5 ft) and width between 12 and 14 meters (39 and 46 feet).
The home-straight extends uphill and is 225 meters (738 ft) long.

The Canons Regular of the Holy Sepulchre were a Catholic religious order of canons regular of the Rule of Saint Augustine said to have been founded in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, then the capital of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, recognised in 1113 by Papal bull of Pope Paschal II.
Other accounts has it that they were founded earlier, during the rule of Godfrey of Bouillon (1099–1100).
The Week is a weekly British news magazine founded in 1995 which has also published a US edition since 2001 and a children's edition in the UK since 2015.
Between 2008 and 2012 it also published an Australian edition.
Nominative determinism is the hypothesis that people tend to gravitate towards areas of work that fit their names.
The term was first used in the magazine "New Scientist" in 1994, after the magazine's humorous Feedback column noted several studies carried out by researchers with remarkably fitting surnames.
These included a book on polar explorations by Daniel Snowman and an article on urology by researchers named Splatt and Weedon.
These and other examples led to light-hearted speculation that some sort of psychological effect was at work.
Since the term appeared, nominative determinism has been an irregularly recurring topic in "New Scientist", as readers continue to submit examples.
Nominative determinism differs from the related concept aptronym, and its synonyms aptonym, namephreak, and Perfect Fit Last Name, in that it focusses on causality.
"Aptronym" merely means the name is fitting, without saying anything about why it has come to fit.
The Gloucester Old Bank was a British bank that operated between 1716 and 1838.
It was founded in 1716 by James Wood.
The bank was said to have been the oldest private bank in Britain, having survived the financial consequences of the Napoleonic Wars when many other banks went out of business.
The claim was wrong as both C. Hoare & Co and Child & Co. were founded earlier, it was, however, one of the oldest banks in Britain in the nineteenth century.
New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.
Since 1996 it has also run a website.
The 1883 Philadelphia Quakers season was the first in the franchise's existence.
The team was founded earlier in the year as a replacement for the Worcester franchise.
It was the first year Philadelphia was represented in the National League since the original Athletics were disbanded in 1876.
The American Association's Philadelphia Athletics had been founded a year earlier.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Which was founded earlier, The Week or New Scientist?
hop1_docs: New Scientist | New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.  Since 1996 it has also run a website.

The New Times (magazine) | The New Times (Russian: Но́вые Времена́ ) is a Russian language magazine in Russia.  The magazine was founded in 1943.  The current version, established in 1988, , is a liberal, independent Russian weekly news magazine, publishing for Russia and Armenia.  (During the Soviet times it was a multi-language political magazine which followed the official party line.)  Its chief editor is Russian investigative journalist, political scientist, writer and radio host Yevgenia Albats.

Bat Boy (character) | Bat Boy is a creature who made several appearances in the American supermarket tabloid "Weekly World News".  "The Weekly World News" published patently fabricated stories which were purported to be factual.  Within the pages of the paper, Bat Boy is described as a creature who is 'half human and half bat'.  His pursuers, according to "Weekly World News", are scientists and United States government officials; he is frequently captured, then later makes a daring escape.  The original scientist who found him was named Dr. Ron Dillon.  Matthew Daemon, S.O.S. (Seeker of Obscure Supernaturals) crossed paths with him on several occasions.

Vinod Agarwal | Dr. Vinod K. Agarwal is an Indian-American businessman and scientist.  He was a professor at McGill University in Montreal, Canada from 1978 to 1992.  He then founded LogicVision, which he took public in October, 2001, as the first technology IPO following the terrorist attacks earlier that year.  Agarwal left LogicVision in 2005 to start SemIndia, a semiconductor company based in India.

Odisha Sampark Kranti Express | Odisha Sampark Kranti Express is a bi-weekly train which runs between Bhubaneswar,Capital of Odisha and Anand Vihar Terminal.  This train was introduced as part of the Sampark Kranti trains that were announced by the then railway minister Nitish Kumar in 2005.  It was and still is a bi-weekly train.  Now there is a requirement of attaching AC First class berths to this train.  Earlier the train used to terminate at New Delhi railway station (NDLS) but now terminates at Anand Vihar Terminus (ANVT) of New Delhi.  Its rakes are maintained by East Coast Railway (ECoR).

The Aquarian Weekly | The Aquarian Weekly is a regional alternative weekly newspaper based in New Jersey.  Founded in 1969, its focus is rock music and related events in the New Jersey/New York City/Eastern Pennsylvania region.  From 1986 to 1992, it was called East Coast Rocker.  After returning to its original title, the newspaper began including a pull-out section that retained the "East Coast Rocker" name, and which is now freely distributed throughout the region.

New Adventures | New Adventures is a British dance company, specialising in contemporary dance.  Founded by the choreographer Matthew Bourne in 2002, the company developed from an earlier company Adventures in Motion Pictures, which was later dissolved.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - New Scientist: Passage [1] states New Scientist is "founded in 1956." (passage [1])
  - The Week: No passage among [1]–[7] mentions "The Week" or gives any founding-year tokens.
- Ambiguities:
  - The Week page/variant is missing from the provided passages. It may have multiple editions/variants (e.g., different-country editions), but no candidate titles appear in the passages to disambiguate.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: "The Week" (the magazine named in the question).
  - Missing fact needed: the founding year (or founding date) of The Week (which exact edition/version if multiple).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "The Week (magazine)" page (or "The Week (disambiguation)" if present); check the lede/infobox and "History" or "Founding" section for the founding year/date and country/edition details. Search keywords: The Week magazine founded year.
  2) Open "The Week (US)" / "The Week (India)" / other edition pages if a disambiguation page appears, and check lede/infobox for founding year (to resolve which edition matches the question).
  3) Re-open "New Scientist" page lede/infobox to confirm the quoted founding year ("founded in 1956") and ensure comparable citation.
Final answer deferred until The Week's founding-year page/section is retrieved and compared to New Scientist's founding year.
response.hop2_query: Founding years of The Week and New Scientist?
hop2_docs: New Scientist | New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.  Since 1996 it has also run a website.

Church of Christ, Scientist | The Church of Christ, Scientist was founded in 1879 in Boston, Massachusetts, by Mary Baker Eddy, author of "Science and Health with Key to the Scriptures," and founder of Christian Science.  The church was founded "to commemorate the word and works of [Christ Jesus]" and "reinstate primitive Christianity and its lost element of healing".  Sunday services are held throughout the year and weekly testimony meetings are held on Wednesday evenings, where following brief readings from the Bible and the Christian Science textbook, those in attendance are invited to give testimonies of healing brought about through Christian Science prayer.

The New Worker | The New Worker is the weekly newspaper produced by the New Communist Party of Britain (NCP).  Its first edition came out a few weeks after the NCP was founded in July 1977.  For the first two years the paper was commercially printed but in 1979 production became entirely in-house with the purchase of an off-set litho press.

New York Fashion Week | New York Fashion Week, held in February and September of each year, is a semi-annual series of events (generally lasting 7–9 days) when international fashion collections are shown to buyers, the press and the general public.  It is one of four major fashion weeks in the world, collectively known as the "Big 4," along with those in Paris, London and Milan.  The Council of Fashion Designers of America (CFDA) created the modern notion of a centralized “New York Fashion Week” in 1993, although cities like London were already using their city’s name in conjunction with the words “fashion week” in the 1980s.  NYFW is based on a much older series of events called “Press Week,” founded in 1942.

The New Times (magazine) | The New Times (Russian: Но́вые Времена́ ) is a Russian language magazine in Russia.  The magazine was founded in 1943.  The current version, established in 1988, , is a liberal, independent Russian weekly news magazine, publishing for Russia and Armenia.  (During the Soviet times it was a multi-language political magazine which followed the official party line.)  Its chief editor is Russian investigative journalist, political scientist, writer and radio host Yevgenia Albats.

Bat Boy (character) | Bat Boy is a creature who made several appearances in the American supermarket tabloid "Weekly World News".  "The Weekly World News" published patently fabricated stories which were purported to be factual.  Within the pages of the paper, Bat Boy is described as a creature who is 'half human and half bat'.  His pursuers, according to "Weekly World News", are scientists and United States government officials; he is frequently captured, then later makes a daring escape.  The original scientist who found him was named Dr. Ron Dillon.  Matthew Daemon, S.O.S. (Seeker of Obscure Supernaturals) crossed paths with him on several occasions.

Bill Pickering (rocket scientist) | William Hayward "Bill" Pickering {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} (24 December 1910 – 15 March 2004) was a New Zealand-born rocket scientist who headed Pasadena, California's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) for 22 years, retiring in 1976.  He was a senior NASA luminary and pioneered the exploration of space.  Pickering was also a founding member of the United States National Academy of Engineering.
response.summary_2: New Scientist was founded in 1956. The provided passages do not specify the founding year of The Week (the magazine), so which was founded earlier cannot be determined from these sources.
response.answer: Cannot determine; The Week's founding year is not provided in the summaries.
